title: St. Eusebius of Samosata, St. Peter the Confessor, St. Cosmas of Chalcedon & St. Athanasia of Aegina
primary_saint: St. Eusebius of Samosata the Confessor-Bishop, St. Peter the Confessor, St. Cosmas of Chalcedon, and St. Athanasia of Aegina
narrative:
On the eighteenth of Miazia, the holy Church gathers four witnesses under the theme of concealment and revelation — of holiness that operates in disguise, that does its work under cover of ordinary appearance, and that is revealed as extraordinary only in retrospect or by grace. Saint Eusebius of Samosata is the great disguised missionary of the Arian crisis: when the Nicene bishops were being systematically deposed and exiled by the Arian emperors, Eusebius of Samosata travelled through the eastern provinces in the disguise of a soldier, moving from community to community to ordain new Nicene clergy, strengthen the faith of wavering bishops, and maintain the apostolic succession in communities that had been left without orthodox leadership. His disguise was not cowardice but strategy: the work required a live Eusebius rather than a martyred one, and he judged correctly that the disguise served the mission better than the public confrontation. He was eventually martyred anyway — struck by a brick thrown from a rooftop by an Arian woman.

Saint Peter the Confessor is a witness of the Egyptian church whose particular form of the confessional vocation involved the specific suffering of a pastor who is imprisoned while his community continues to need him: he could hear the prayers and the liturgy of the church he governed being celebrated on the other side of the prison wall, could communicate with his community only through the narrow window or the messages carried by the faithful who brought him food, and sustained his episcopal ministry through the medium of those restrictions that were designed to end it. The tradition honours Peter as a figure of the God who governs the world from the apparent impotence of the Cross, the bishop who pastors from prison as the risen Christ pastors from the tomb's other side.

Saint Cosmas of Chalcedon is this day's discovery of episcopal witness in a contested theological city: a bishop of Chalcedon, that city whose council had split the Oriental churches and whose very name carried the weight of the great Christological controversy, who governed his community with pastoral charity and theological nuance in a period when the Chalcedonian debate made every act of episcopal governance a potential political flashpoint. His ministry of reconciliation in a divided community is honoured in the Miazia illumination as an image of the work that the Resurrection makes possible: the bringing together of those whom controversy has separated, the restoration of communion across the lines of the theological frontier.

Saint Athanasia of Aegina returns for her third appearance in Miazia, because the tradition has more to say about her in the second half of the month than the twelfth day could accommodate. The detail the Miazia illumination most treasures about Athanasia is her return to Aegina from Constantinople: after seven years in the city's convent, she came back to the community she had founded, and the tradition records that her arrival was a kind of resurrection for the community, which had begun to scatter in her absence. She gathered it again, governed it until her death, and died surrounded by the women she had called from widowhood into a new life. Eusebius in soldier's disguise, Peter governing from prison, Cosmas holding Chalcedon together, Athanasia returning from exile to her flock: four images of the pastoral resurrection. Amen.
